Facts.text: Gerbert of Aurillac is elected as the first French Pope
Jewish physician Joseph Hacohen is expelled from the city of Genoa, followed by the expulsion of all Jews soon afterward
England and France sign the first Treaty of Le Cateau-Cambrésis
The States-General appoints Earl Maurice as the Viceroy of Utrecht
Robert Devereux, 3rd Earl of Essex, resigns as Parliament's supreme commander during the English Civil War
Commodore William James captures the pirate fortress of Suvarnadurg on west coast of India
King Charles III of Spain gives orders to expel the Jesuits from the Spanish Empire

The Coinage Act is passed, establishing the United States Mint and authorizing the $10 Eagle, $5 Half-Eagle, $2.50 Quarter-Eagle gold coins, and the silver dollar, half dollar, quarter, dime, and half-dime
Forty merchantmen are wrecked when a convoy led by HMS Apollo runs aground off Portugal.
1st successful agricultural journal ("American Farmer") first publishes
US inventor Joseph Dixon of Salem, Massachusetts, begins manufacturing lead pencils
H L Fizeau & Leon Foucault take 1st photo of the Sun
1st Italian Parliament meets at Turin
Bread revolt in Richmond, Virginia
Skirmish at Crump's Hill (Piney Woods), Louisiana
Skirmish at Spoonville, Antoine, Arkansas
Battle of Fort Blakely Alabama, last major battle of the US Civil War
Battle of Petersburg ends as the Army of Northern Virginia is forced to retreat under heavy fire from a general Union offensive
Battle of Selma, Alabama, Union forces break Confederates defenses to secure the town
US engineer George Brayton patents an internal combustion engine (Brayton Cycle)
1st Easter egg roll held on White House lawn
1st issue of Rotterdam's Newspaper
Battle at Bamako: French assault on Fabous arm forces attack
London prison for debtors closed
1st edition of The Volk published (Amsterdam)
Dmitry Sipyagin, Minister of Interior of the Russian Empire, is assassinated by a terrorist in the Marie Palace, St Petersburg
Dutch football club Maatschappelijke Voetbal Vereniging (MVV) is established in Maastricht, Netherlands
Electric Theatre, the first full-time movie theater in the United States, opens in Los Angeles, California
Cairo-Capetown railway opens
Dave Nourse takes 4 wickets and Reggie Schwarz 3 as South Africa wins the 5th cricket Test in Cape Town to complete a 4-1 series drubbing of England
Sun Yet Sen forms Guomindang Party in China
57 armed New Zealand police invade the remote Ngāi Tūhoe settlement of Maungapōhatu in the Urewera Ranges to arrest the Māori prophet Rua Kēnana
German troops overtake Bois de Caillette
Riots between Muslims & Hindus in Calcutta
1st NY-Bermuda airplane flight lands in Bermuda
Svenska Aeroplan Aktiebolaget (SAAB) is founded in Trollhättan, Sweden
German occupier disallows Dutch scouting association
LPGA Titleholders Championship Women's Golf, Augusta CC: Dorothy Kirby retains title by 5 strokes ahead of Eileen Stulb
Soviet Army marches into pro-German Romania
Diplomatic relations between the Soviet Union and Brazil are established
Carlo Terron's "Il diamente del profeta" premieres in Rome
WTAR (now WTKR) TV channel 3 in Norfolk, VA (CBS) begins broadcasting
Raab forms his 1st government in Austria
Ed Wood's cult classic film "Glen or Glenda?", initially screened as "I Changed My Sex" premieres in San Francisco, California
Plans to build Disneyland 1st announced [see Jan 26]
Soap operas "As the World Turns" & "Edge of Night" premiere on TV
Antillean Brewery (Amstel beer) opens
Wind speed reaches a record 450 kph in tornado, Wichita Falls, Texas
Cuba buys oil from USSR
KPEC TV channel 56 in Lakewood Center-Tacoma, WA (PBS) 1st broadcast
Mabry Harper catches a world record 25lb Walleye at Cedar Bluff on the Cumberland River in Tennessee
"Eye of the Needle", a revolving restaurant, opens atop the Space Needle at the Century 21 Exposition (World's Fair) in Seattle, Washington; designed by architect John Graham, Jr
First official panda crossing opens outside Waterloo Station in London
Explorer 17 attains Earth orbit (254/914 km)
USSR launches Luna 4; missed Moon by 8,500 km
USSR launches Zond 1 to Venus; no data returned
Hochhuths play "Stellvertreter" banned in Italy
Soviet Union's Luna 10 becomes 1st spacecraft to orbit Moon
WJET TV channel 24 in Erie, PA (ABC) begins broadcasting
Beatles form Python Music Ltd
Chad creates Union of Central African States

Toronto center Forbes Kennedy sets a Stanley Cup playoff record for most penalties in one game with 8 as the Maple Leafs crash 10-0 to the Bruins at Boston; Boston’s first NHL playoff victory in 10 years
2 men begin ascent of south face of Annapurna I, highest final stage in a wall climb in world
Meghalaya becomes autonomous state within India's Assam state
Dark Shadows, American Gothic supernatural soap opera, concludes an almost 5 year run on ABC
Libya concludes 5 weeks of negotiations with Western oil companies in Tripoli
CBS radio begins on the hour news, 24 hours a day
ITT admits to asking CIA to influence Chilean presidential election
Launch of the LexisNexis computerized legal research service.
Arganat Comm publishes report concerning Yom Kippur War
Vietnam War: Thousands of civilian refugees flee from the Quang Ngai Province in front of advancing North Vietnamese troops.
Cambodia Khieu Sampan succeeds Prince Sihanouk as Premier
Portuguese constitution assumed
Montreal Canadiens rout the Washington Capitals, 11-0 at the Montreal Forum to set an NHL record of 34 straight home games without a loss
Georges de Mestral's patent for "velvet type fabric" expires, puts "Velcro" in the open market
Jamaican batsman Basil Williams scores 100 on debut for the West Indies in Australia's 3 wicket 3rd Test victory in Georgetown, Guyana
Belgium's 4th government of Martens resigns
Heavy battle between Christian militia & Syrian army in East Lebanon
Several thousand Argentine troops invade and seize the Falkland Islands from the United Kingdom
Edmonton centerWayne Gretzkysets an NHL record with his 34th career hat trick as the Oilers beat the Kings 6-4 in Los Angeles
The NCAA Rules Committee adopts a 45-second shot clock for men’s basketball to begin in the 1986 season in an effort to eliminate stalling
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
4 US passengers killed by bomb at TWA counter Athens Airport Greece
George Corley Wallace (Gov-D-Ala) announces retirement plans
NCAA Basketball Rules Committee votes to adopt the 3-point shot beginning the 1986-87 season; sets college distance at 19 feet, 9 inches, compared to 21 feet in the NBA
IBM introduces PS/2 & OS/2
Calgary Flames right wing Joe Mullen sets new NHL record for most points in a season by a American-born player, with a goal and 2 assists in a 4-2 win over Edmonton Oilers gives him 110 points
Rotterdam Daily Newspaper begins publishing
Edith Cresson, France's 1st female prime minister, resigns
Space Shuttle STS-45 (Atlantis 11) lands
1st test flight of Fokker 70 (Amsterdam)
Cleveland Cavaliers guard Mark Price has his consecutive free throw streak end at 77, during a 114-113 loss at Charlotte; falls 1 short of Calvin Murphy’s NBA record of 78 straight free throws
Venezuelan DC-10 crashes at Margarita, killing 10

Longest strike in American major league sports history (232 days) ends as MLB owners accept players’ offer to return to work without a contract; MLB is the first major pro sports league to lose an entire postseason due to a labor dispute
North & Western Colorado begins using new area code 970
NY Police Department & NY Transit Police merge into one organization
Sunday NY Times raises price from $2.00 to $2.50
Vancouver Grizzlies beat Minnesota Timberwolves, 105-103 at General Motors Place Arena to end their NBA single-season record 23-game consecutive loss streak
"Doll's House" opens at Belasco Theater NYC

Adam Gilchrist takes his 10th catch in the 3rd cricket Test against New Zealand in Hamilton to create an Australian record for dismissals by a wicketkeeper; Australia wins by 6 wickets for 3-0 series sweep
Atlanta center Ray Ferraro scores a goal and added 2 assists as the Thrashers end an NHL record 17-game home winless streak (0-15-2) with a 5-4 win against the visiting New York Islanders
Israeli forces surround the Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem into which armed Palestinians had retreated. A siege ensues.
Islamist terrorists involved in the 11 March 2004 Madrid attacks attempt to bomb the Spanish high-speed train AVE near Madrid. Their attack is thwarted.
Bubba Stewart becomes first African-American to win a major motor sports event when he takes out the Monster Energy AMA Supercross C'ship event in Irving, Texas
Newcastle United teammates Kieron Dyer and Lee Bowyer are sent off for fighting each other in a 3-0 home defeat to EPL rivals Aston Villa at St. James’ Park
Over 60 tornadoes break out, hardest hit is Tennessee with 29 people killed.
Oikos University, Oakland shooting kills seven people and injures 3
13 children are killed in a fire in a mosque in Yangon, Burma
13 people are killed in a quarry accident, in Arusha, Tanzania
4 more critical cases of bird flu (H7N9) are reported in China
7 people are killed in an attack on a power plant in Peshawar, Pakistan
9 mutilated bodies are found in an SUV in Tamaulipas, Mexico
Eurozone unemployment reaches a high of 12%
The UN General Assembly approves the first Arms Trade Treaty
Uruguay passes legislation to legalize same-sex marriage
140 people are killed after gunmen attack Garissa University College, Kenya
Attack on visitors to Muslim shrine by a custodian and others in Sargodha, Pakistan leaves 20 dead
70 villages evacuated in Khuzesta province, Iran, after at least 45 killed in flooding after unprecedented rainfall
Canada is warming at twice the speed of the rest of the world, according to a federal report, increasing 2.3% in northern Canada
Lori Lightfoot elected first female African American Mayor of Chicago
NASA states it wants to send astronauts to Mars by 2033, and land on the Moon again in 2024
OKC guard Russell Westbrook becomes 2nd player in NBA history to have 20+ points, rebounds and assists in a game; records 20-20-21 in 119-103 win over LA Lakers
New study shows western Antarctica once swampy with temperate forests 93-83 million years ago during Cretaceous period, according to Alfred Wegener Institute
Record 6.6 million Americans file claims for unemployment in the prior week according to the US Department of Labor, 10 million in the prior two weeks
Spain's death toll passes 10,000 (10003), as it posts a record single-day rise of 950 deaths
New study suggests the asteroid that wiped out the dinosaurs 66 million years ago created South America's tropical rainforests
Ukraine liberates the entire Kyiv region from retreating Russian forces
Footage of deepest living fish ever recorded, the snailfish, released caught on camera at depth of 27,349 feet (8,336 meters) in the Izu-Ogasawara Trench, south of Japan
Lake Tulare, once the largest body of water west of the Mississippi, re-emerges after a series of storms in California's Central Valley to cover 160 square miles
Bassirou Diomaye Faye takes office as President of Senegal and Africa's youngest elected President at 44
Portuguese center-right minority government sworn in, led by Prime Minister Luis Montenegro
Powerful spring storms begin crossing the US Midwest and mid-South, killing at least 23 people in Kentucky, Arkansas, Tennessee and Missouri
Alfonso Vsucceeds his father as King of Aragon
ExplorerJuan Ponce de Leónclaims Florida for Spain as the first known European to reach it
Cornelis de Houtman's ships depart Holland for Asia via the Cape of Good Hope on the 1st Dutch Expedition to the East Indies (Indonesia)
William Cavendish-Bentinckbecomes Prime Minister of Great Britain after an opposition coalition of Henry Fox and Frederick North forcesWilliam Pettyto resign
1st performance ofLudwig van Beethoven's 1st Symphony in C
Confederate PresidentJefferson Davisflees Confederate capital of Richmond, Virginia (US Civil War)
US PresidentAndrew Johnsonends civil war in Alabama, Arkansas, Florida, Georgia, Mississippi, Louisiana, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee & Virginia
Sixth Impressionist Exhibition opens in Paris organized byEdgar Degasand showing his famous "Little Dancer of Fourteen Years", the only sculpture shown in his lifetime
RMS Titanicundergoes sea trials under its own power
Jeannette Rankin(Rep-R-Montana) begins her term as 1st woman member of US House of Reps
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonasks Congress to declare war against Germany
Albert Einsteinlectures in New York City on his new "Theory of Relativity"
Ras Tafari Makonnen is proclaimed EmperorHaile Selassieof Abyssinia (Ethiopia)
17-year old girl Jackie Mitchell strikes out New York Yankees starsBabe RuthandLou Gehrigin an exhibition baseball game at Engel Stadium in Chattanooga, Tennessee
"Tarzanthe Ape Man" released starring Olympic swimmerJohnny Weissmullerin the first of his 12 Tarzan films
Charles Lindberghturns over $50,000 as ransom for his kidnapped son
Scottish physicistRobert Watson-Wattreceives a British patents for RADAR
USS Hornet with Jimmy Doolittles B-25 departs from San Francisco
Dmitri Shostakovich's 8th Symphony premieres in NY
Palmiro Togliatti, leader of the Italian Communist Party returns to Italy from the Soviet Union
Peter Ustinovs' play "Romanoff & Juliet" premieres in Manchester
"2001A Space Odyssey" directed byStanley Kubrickand starring Keir Dullea and Gary Lockwood, premieres at the Uptown Theater in Washington, D.C.
SenatorEugene McCarthywins Democratic primary in Wisconsin
New York Mets managerGil Hodgescollapses just minutes after completing a round of golf in West Palm Beach, Florida, and dies of a heart attack
Tennessee Williams' "Small Craft Warnings" premieres in NYC
46th Academy Awards: "The Sting",Glenda Jackson&Jack Lemmonwin

Oakland Athletics trade 2 key members from recent World Series Championship team, sending outfielderReggie Jacksonand pitcherKen Holtzmanto Baltimore Orioles in exchange for outfielder Don Baylor and pitchers Mike Torrez and Paul Mitchell
Fleetwood Mac's "Rumours" album goes to No. 1 and stays atop the charts for 31 weeks
Czech tennis starMartina Navratilovawins her first WTA Tour Championship; beatsEvonne Goolagong Cawleyof Australia 7-6, 6-4 at Oakland Coliseum Arena
TV show "Dallas" starringLarry Hagmanand Barbara Bel Geddes premieres on CBS as a 5 week mini-series; ratings success leads to a 13 year run
Israeli Prime MinisterMenachem Beginmeets Egyptian PresidentAnwar Sadatin Cairo, Egypt
In his first full season in the NHL,Wayne Gretzkyscores for Edmonton in a 1-1 tie with Minnesota North Stars to become the youngest player to reach 50 goals at 19 years and 2 months of age
Montreal right wingGuy Lafleurscores twice to become the first player in NHL history to record 6 straight 50-goal seasons, as the Canadiens beat the Red Wings, 7-2 at Detroit
New York Islanders right wingMike Bossyscores in a 6-3 win over the Pittsburgh Penguins to become the first player in NHL history to score 60 goals in 3 consecutive seasons
Quebec's Czech centerPeter Šťastnýscores his 100th NHL point of the season for the 5th straight year, with an assist for the Nordiques in a 6-4 win over the Boston Bruins in Quebec City
NYC MayorEd Kochsigns & brings the Gay Rights Bill into effect
Paul Coffeyof the Edmonton Oilers scores his 47th and 48th goals of the season in an 8-4 win over the Vancouver Canucks to break Bobby Orr's NHL record for most goals by a defenseman
West Indies cricket fast bowling greatCurtly Ambrosemakes his debut in 9 wicket 1st Test defeat to Pakistan in Georgetown, Guyana; low key debut with 2-108

Mafia bossJohn Gottiis found guilty of 5 murders (Paul Castellano, Thomas Bilotti, Robert DiBernardo, Liborio Milito and Louis Dibono), plus conspiracy to murder, loansharking, illegal gambling, obstruction of justice, bribery and tax evasion
Capitol Records releases "10 Song Demo", the ninth studio album by American singer-songwriterRosanne Cash. co-produced by Cash and husband John Leventhal
Detroit Tigers sluggerCecil Fieldersteals 1st base in 1,097th career game, a 10-6 win at Minnesota Twins; longest duration in MLB history without a stolen base
Sri Lankan cricket batsmanSanath Jayasuriyahits century in 48 balls (134 off 65), in 34 run win over Pakistan in Singapore; world ODI record
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, GC at Desert Mountain:Tom Kitewins his lone Champions Tour major with birdie on 6th playoff hole withTom Watson&Larry Nelson
New York Yankees pitcherRoger Clemensbecomes American League all-time strikeout leader, recording his 3,509th K in a 7-3 victory over the Kansas City Royals; Walter Johnson previous record 3,508 Ks
Seattle outfielderIchiro Suzukihas 2 hits and becomes first Japanese position player to play in a regular season MLB game as Mariners beat Oakland A's, 5-4 at Safeco Field
At 27 years, 249 days Texas Rangers infielderAlex Rodriguezbecomes the youngest MLB player to hit 300 home runs; homers off Ramon Ortiz in 5th inning of an 11-5 loss to the Angels in Anaheim
Laureus World Sports Awards, Palau Sant Jordi, Barcelona, Spain: Sportsman:Roger Federer; Sportswoman: Yelena Isinbayeva; Team: Italy Men's National Football team
Basketball superstarKobe Bryantsigns a three-year contract extension with the NBA's Los Angeles Lakers worth $87 million
ICC Men's Cricket ODI World Cup, Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai, India: India beats Sri Lanka by 6 wickets for their second title; Player of the Match:MS Dhoni(IND) 91 no (79)
Algerian PresidentAbdelaziz Bouteflika(82) resigns after 20 years in office after widespread protests over running for another term
Brazilian presidentJair Bolsonarodownplays the COVID-19 pandemic saying "its not all its being made out to be"
Finnish parliamentary elections give the center right National Coalition Party led byPetteri Orpoa slender advantage, defeating PM Sanna Marin's center left Social Democrats without a clear majority
There are 141 more billionaires in the world in 2024, 2,781 in total according to Forbes, includingTaylor Swiftfor the first time
US PresidentDonald Trumpannounces "Liberation Day", unveiling wide-ranging tariffs on foreign countries importing into the US, including 34% on China and 20% for the European Union
Battle of Copenhagen: British naval forces led byHoratio Nelsondestroy the Danish fleet during the Napoleonic Wars
1st human cannonball act performed by 14-year-old Rossa Matilda Richter known as Zazel at the Royal Aquarium in London
Number of COVID-19 cases worldwide passes 1 million, with 1,002,159 cases and 51,485 deaths reported, according to Johns Hopkins University
Igor Svyatoslavich, Ukrainian Prince of Novgorod-Seversk (1180-98) and Prince of Chernigov (1198-1202), born in Novgorod-Seversk (d. 1202)
Ashikaga Yoshiharu, 12th Japanese shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1521-45), born in Japan (d. 1550)
Maria de Jesus de Agreda [Maria Coronel], Spanish franciscan, born in Agreda, Spain (d. 1665)
Francesco Maria Grimaldi, Italian mathematician and physicist (light defraction), born in Bologna, Italy (d. 1663)
Constantin Christian Dedekind, German composer and poet, born in Reinsdorf, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1715)
Georg Caspar Weckler, German organist and composer, baptized in Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1695)
Prince George of Denmark, Prince Consort of Anne of England, born in Copenhagen Castle, Denmark (d. 1708)
Jan Punt, Dutch engraver, painter, illustrator and actor (Gideonschool), born in Amsterdam (d. 1779)
Onno Zwier van Haren, Frisian poet (Fatherland), born in St. Annaparochie, Netherlands (d. 1779)
Franz Asplmayr, Austrian composer, born in Linz, Austria (d. 1786)
Giacomo Tritto, Italian composer, born in Altamura, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1824)
Christian Gotthilf Tag, German composer, born in Beierfeld, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1811)
Franz Volrath Buttstett, German composer, born in Erfurt, Electorate of Mainz, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1814)
Armand-Gaston Camus, French revolutionary and Chairman of the Council of 500, born in Paris (d. 1804)
Giacomo Gotifredo Ferrari, Italian composer, baptized in Rovereto, Prince-Bishopric of Trent (d. 1842)
Bhagwan Swaminarayan, Indian yogi and religious leader, born in Chhapaiya, Uttar Pradesh, India (d. 1830)
Francisco Balagtas, Filipino poet (Florante at Laura), born in Barrio Panginay, Balagtas, Bulacan (d. 1862)
August Heinrich Hoffmann, German writer, born in Fallersleben, Electorate of Brunswick -Lüneburg (d. 1874)
Franz Paul Lachner, German composer, born in Rain, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1890)
Giacomo Antonelli, Italian cardinal and assistant secretary of state of Pius IX, born in Sonnino, Italy (d. 1876)
Erastus Brigham Bigelow, American inventor of weaving machine for carpets, born in West Boylston, Massachusetts (d. 1879)
Henry L. Benning, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Columbia County, Georgia (d. 1875)
Teodulo Mabellini, Italian composer, born in Pistoia, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1897)
Philip Dale Roddey, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Moulton, Alabama (d. 1897)
William Holman Hunt, English painter (Light of the World), born in Cheapside, London (d. 1910)
Thomas Howard Ruger, American lawyer and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Livingston County, New York (d. 1907)
Tommy Bond, Irish baseball pitcher and right fielder (Triple Crown 1877; Boston Red Caps; first man born in Ireland to play MLB), born in Granard, Ireland (d. 1941)
Nicholas Murray Butler, American philosopher, diplomat (Nobel Peace Prize 1931), educator (Columbia University President), born in Elizabeth, New Jersey (d. 1947)
Mabel Cahill, Irish tennis player (US National C'ship 1891-92), born in Ballyragget, County Kilkenny (d. 1905)
Eugen Sandow, German bodybuilder and circus performer, born in Königsberg, Germany (d. 1925)
Eduard Jacobs, Dutch cabaret artist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1914)
Hughie Jennings, American Baseball Hall of Fame infielder (Baltimore Orioles) and manager (Detroit Tigers 1907-20), born in Pittston, Pennsylvania (d. 1928)
Marshall Newell, American College Football Hall of Fame tackle (Harvard; All-American 1890-93) and coach (Cornell, Tufts), born in Springfield, Massachusetts (d. 1897)
William Donne, English cricket batsman, (Olympic gold 1900) and administrator (President England Rugby Football Union), born in Wincanton, England (d. 1934)
Arnold Schering, German musicologist (Humor, Heldentum, Tragik), born in Breslau, Germany (d. 1941)
Émilie Charmy, French avant-garde artist, born in Saint-Etienne, France (d. 1974)
Harold Weekes, American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (Columbia University; All American 1901), born in Oyster Bay, New York (d. 1950)
Sir J. C. Squire, British poet, writer, and historian, born in Plymouth, Devon (d. 1958)
Max Ernst, German-French surrealist painter and sculptor (founded surrealism), born in Brühl, German Empire (d. 1976)
Rudolph Cleveringa, Dutch anti-nazi lawyer and professor of Law at Leiden University, born in Appingedam, Netherlands (d. 1980)
Johnny Golden, American golfer (PGA C'ship 1922, 26, 27 3rd; Ryder Cup 1927, 29 [3-0-0 record]; 9 x PGA Tour titles), born in Tuxedo, New York (d. 1936)
Alfred Strange, English soccer right half (20 caps, 3 as captain; Sheffield Wednesday, 253 games), born in Ripley, England (d. 1978)
Anis Fuleihan, Lebanese-American classical pianist, composer (Theremin Concerto), conductor, and pedagogue, born in Kyrenia, British Cyprus (d. 1970)
Heinrich Besseler, German musicologist, born in Hörde, Germany (d. 1969)
Roberto Arlt, Argentine writer (The Seven Madmen), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1942)
Danilo Švara, Slovenian pianist, composer (Ocean; Rebellious Cantata), conductor, and educator (Ljubljana Academy, 1932-72), born in San Giuseppe, Trieste, Italy (d. 1981)
Jan Tschichold, German typographer, born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1974)
Lionel Chevrier, Canadian politician (Canadian Member of Parliament), born in Cornwall, Ontario (d. 1987)
Edmond Jouhaud, French general, born in Aïn Boucefar, French Algeria (d. 1995)
Kurt Alfred Adler, Austrian therapist and writer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1997)
Serge Lifar, Russian-French ballet dancer (Ballet Russes, 1923-29), ballet master (Paris Opera Ballet, 1930-44 and 1947-58), and choreographer, born in Kyiv, Russian Empire (d. 1986)
Harald Andersson, Swedish discus thrower (world record 52.42m 1934), born in Stanford, California (d. 1985)
Luke Appling, American Baseball Hall of Fame shortstop (7 x MLB All Star; AL batting champion 1936, 43; Chicago White Sox), born in High Point, North Carolina (d. 1991)
Christian "Buddy" Ebsen Jr., American dancer and actor (The Beverly Hillbillies - "Jed Clampett"; Breakfast At Tiffany's; Barnaby Jones), born in Belleville, Illinois (d. 2003)
Jean Kurt Forest, German composer, born in Darmstadt, Germany (d. 1975)
Arnie Herber, American Pro Football Hall of Fame quarterback (NFL C'ship 1930, 31, 36, 39; First-team All-Pro 1932, 35, 36; Green Bay Packers, NY Giants), born in Green Bay, Wisconsin (d. 1969)
Irene Mayer Selznick, American socialite and theatrical producer (Tennessee Williams' "A Streetcar Named Desire"), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1990)
Paul Triquet, Canadian military officer, born in Cabano, Quebec (d. 1980)
Charles "Honi" Coles, American actor and tap dancer (Tito Suarez-Dirty Dancing), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1992)
Herbert Mills, American jazz and pop singer and vocal trumpet (The Mills Brothers - "Paper Doll", "Up A Lazy River", "The Glow-Worm"), born in Piqua, Ohio (d. 1989)
Ollie O'Toole, American comedian, impressionist, and actor, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1992)
Benny Lynch, Scottish boxer (World flyweight title 1937-38), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1946)
Al Barlick, American Baseball Hall of Fame umpire (7 x World Series; 7 x MLB All-Star games), born in Springfield, Illinois (d. 1995)
Antony Dornhorst, British physician and Professor of Medicine, born in Woodford, Essex, England (d. 2003)
Arthur Ballard, English artist, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1994)
Lou Monte [Scaglione], Italian-American novelty song singer-songwriter (Peppino the Italian Mouse; Dominick the Donkey), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1989)
Robert "Dabbs" Greer, American actor (Gunsmoke, The Green Mile, Little House on Prairie), born in Fairview, Missouri (d. 2007)
Charles White, American artist (The Contribution of the Negro to American Democracy), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1979)
Antoon Hubben, Dutch Mayor of Maasbree and trade union leader, born in Nieuwenhagen, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Delfo Cabrera, Argentine distance runner (Olympic gold marathon 1948), born in Armstrong, Argentina (d. 1981)
Ian Hunter, British impresario of classical music, born in Hadley Wood, Middlesex, England (d. 2003)
Jack Webb, American screenwriter, director and actor (Dragnet, Sunset Boulevard), born in Santa Monica, California (d. 1982)
Nicolay G. Basov, Soviet physicist who specialized in quantum electronics (laser, maser) and 1964 Nobel laureate, born in Usman, Russia (d. 2001)
Clifford Scott Green, American Federal Judge, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2007)
Gloria Henry [McEniry], American actress (Dennis the Menace), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2021)[1]
Jacob Firet, Dutch theologian (Dynamics in Pastoring), born in the Netherlands (d. 1994)[1]
Bobby Ávila, Mexican Baseball Hall of Fame second baseman (MLB All-Star 1952, 54, 55; AL batting champion 1954; Cleveland Indians), born in Veracruz City, Mexico (d. 2004)
Denis Rooke, British engineer and CEO (British Gas), born in New Cross, London (d. 2008)
Wallace "Bud" Smith, American boxer (World lightweight title 1955-56), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1973)
George MacDonald Fraser, British poet and author (Flashman at the Charge), born in Carlisle, England (d. 2008)

Hard Boiled Haggerty [Don Stansauk], American pro wrestler (AWA, NWA, WWA World Tag Team C'ship; IWA World Heavyweight C'ship) and actor (The Incredible Hulk), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2004)
David Mason, British orchestral, solo and session trumpet player (The Beatles - "Penny Lane"), born in London, England (d. 2011)
Jack Brabham, Australian auto racer and team owner (World F1 champion 1959, 60, 66 [only man to win F1 C'ship driving one of his own cars]), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2014)
Michael Rizzello, English sculptor and coin designer, born in London (d. 2004)

Billy Pierce, American baseball pitcher (MLB All Star x 7; World Series 1945; AL wins leader 1957; MLB ERA leader 1955; AL strikeout leader; 1953; Chicago White Sox), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2015)
Carmen Basilio, American boxer (World Welterweight title 1955-56; Middleweight title 1957-58), born in Canastota, New York (d. 2012)
Des Bartlett [Norman Desmond Bartlett], Australian wildlife photographer and documentarian (The Flight of the Snow Geese, Survivors of the Skeleton Coast), born in Canungra, Australia (d. 2009)
Ferenc Puskás, Hungarian soccer forward (Hungary 85 caps, Spain 4; Real Madrid, Budapest Honvéd) and manager (Panathinaikos), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2006)
Joseph Bernardin, American Cardinal of the Catholic Church, born in Columbia, South Carolina (d. 1996)
Rita Gam, American actress (Distortions, Hannibal), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2016)
Serge Gainsbourg [Lucien Ginsburg], French singer-songwriter, record producer ("Je t'aime... moi non plus"; "Requiem pour un twister"), and actor, born in Paris (d. 1991)[1]
Catherine Gaskin, Irish-Australian romantic novelist (Fiona, Coporation Wife), born in Dundalk Bay, County Louth, Ireland (d. 2009)
Jack Buchanan, British theatre and film actor, singer, dancer, and producer (The Band Wagon), born in Helensburgh, Scotland (d. 1957)
Bill McColl, American College Football Hall of Fame end (Stanford University; Chicago Bears 1952-59), born in San Diego, California (d. 2023)
Girolamo Arriego, Italian composer, born in Palermo, Italy
Rosalind Fox Solomon, American photographer known for her black-and-white portraits (Girl Rising, Mississippi), born in Highland Park, Illinois (d. 2025)[1]
